content.text: From Claude's perspective, MCP tools and direct function-calling tools look
blocks with a name and structured arguments. The difference is entirely on the other side of the tool_use block: who holds the schema, who holds credentials, and how the execution happens.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of direct function calling vs MCP tool calling

content.text: Claude emits the same tool_use block regardless of whether the tool is a direct Python function or an MCP server. The choice between approaches is an architectural decision, not a model decision.
With direct function calling, you define tool schemas in your agent code, implement the functions in the same Python process, and route tool_use blocks to them in your dispatch loop:
The entire system — schema definition, credential, execution logic — lives in one process. This is the simplest approach and the right choice when:
The tool is only used by one agent in one project.
The tool logic is tightly coupled to the agent's business logic.
You're prototyping and not yet sure if the tool will be reused.
With MCP, the tool is implemented in a separate server process. Your agent code only needs to start the server, list its tools, and forward tool_use blocks to it:

import anthropic, asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

async def run():
    # The server process owns WEATHER_API_KEY — agent never sees it
    params = StdioServerParameters(
        command="python", args=["weather_server.py"]
    )
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()

            # Tools are discovered dynamically — no hardcoded schemas here
            tools_result = await session.list_tools()
            tools = [
                {
                    "name": t.name,
                    "description": t.description or "",
                    "input_schema": t.inputSchema,
                }
                for t in tools_result.tools
            ]

            messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather in Tokyo?"}]
            resp = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=512,
                tools=tools, messages=messages,
            )
            for block in resp.content:
                if block.type == "tool_use":
                    result = await session.call_tool(block.name, block.input)
                    print(result.content[0].text)

asyncio.run(run())
) now lives in the server's environment, never visible to the agent or the model. Choose MCP when:
The tool will be used by multiple agents or frameworks.
You want credential isolation — the model process should never touch API keys.
The tool is complex enough to deserve its own test suite and deployment.
You're building something that should appear in Claude Desktop or Claude Code without additional agent code.
Because Claude sees identical tool_use blocks either way, several things are unchanged:
fields have the same structure. MCP schemas are just fetched dynamically instead of hardcoded.
is identical. Only the dispatch implementation differs.
— both approaches support Claude's parallel tool_use blocks in a single response.
— you can test Claude's tool-calling behavior with either approach using the same mocked tool responses.
When a direct tool grows to the point where it should be extracted to MCP, the migration is mechanical:
Move the function body into an MCP server's
Replace the schema in the agent with a

# BEFORE: Direct function-calling tool
TOOLS = [
    {
        "name": "search_docs",
        "description": "Search internal documentation.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
            "required": ["query"],
        },
    }
]

def search_docs(query: str) -> str:
    # Calls internal API; API key in agent env
    resp = requests.get(DOCS_API, params={"q": query},
                        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['DOCS_KEY']}"},
                        timeout=10)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return "\n".join(r["snippet"] for r in resp.json()["results"][:5])


# AFTER: Identical tool surfaced via MCP
# The agent code becomes:

async def run_with_mcp():
    params = StdioServerParameters(command="python", args=["docs_server.py"])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = [...]  # fetched from session.list_tools()
            # ...same agent loop as before

# docs_server.py (the extracted server):
# @app.call_tool()
# async def call_tool(name, arguments):
#     if name == "search_docs":
#         resp = requests.get(DOCS_API, params={"q": arguments["query"]},
#                             headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['DOCS_KEY']}"},
#                             timeout=10)
#         return [types.TextContent(type="text", text="\n".join(...))]
The agent's Claude-facing behavior is unchanged — same tool name, same input schema, same output format. Only the hosting boundary moves.

Direct tools and MCP tools can coexist in the same agent. A common pattern is to use MCP for third-party services (where credential isolation and reusability matter) while keeping lightweight, agent-specific helpers as direct functions:

The dispatch logic is the only place that distinguishes the two: check the tool name against a set of MCP tool names and route accordingly. Everything else — the Claude API call, the message loop, the tool_result format — is identical.
Direct tools are pure Python functions — test them as unit tests with no mocking. MCP tools require mocking the ClientSession at the boundary:

# Testing both approaches in the same test suite
import asyncio
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock

# Test direct tools: simple unit test, no mocking needed
def test_format_as_markdown():
    result = format_as_markdown('Hello world', 'My Title')
    assert result.startswith('# My Title')
    assert 'Hello world' in result

# Test MCP tools: mock the session
async def test_mcp_list_prs():
    session = AsyncMock()
    session.call_tool.return_value = MagicMock(
        content=[MagicMock(text='PR #1: Add feature', type='text')],
        isError=False,
    )
    result = await session.call_tool('list_pull_requests',
                                      {'owner': 'org', 'repo': 'api'})
    assert 'PR #1' in result.content[0].text

# Test the dispatch routing:
async def test_dispatch_routes_correctly():
    mcp_tool_names = {'list_pull_requests', 'create_issue'}
    direct_tool_names = {'format_as_markdown'}

    # Simulate dispatch logic
    def dispatch(name: str, args: dict) -> str:
        if name in direct_tool_names:
            return format_as_markdown(**args)
        elif name in mcp_tool_names:
            return f'[MCP dispatch: {name}]'
        raise ValueError(f'Unknown tool: {name}')

    assert 'My Title' in dispatch('format_as_markdown', {'text': 'hi', 'title': 'My Title'})
    assert 'MCP dispatch' in dispatch('list_pull_requests', {})
    import pytest
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        dispatch('nonexistent_tool', {})

Keep the dispatch routing logic testable independently of the backends. Injecting the sets of MCP and direct tool names as parameters (rather than globals) makes the routing function easy to unit test.
When adding a new tool, run through four questions:
Will it be used by more than one agent?
If yes, MCP allows sharing without code duplication.
If yes, MCP isolates them in the server process away from the model.
Should it appear in Claude Desktop or Claude Code?
Only MCP servers can do this without custom host code.
Is it written in a different language?
MCP is language-agnostic; direct tools must match the agent's language.
If none of those apply — start direct. Direct tools have zero overhead and can always be migrated to MCP later:

# Decision heuristic — run through these checks when adding a new tool:

def should_use_mcp(tool_name: str,
                   used_by_multiple_agents: bool,
                   has_credentials: bool,
                   needs_claude_desktop: bool,
                   different_language: bool) -> bool:
    """Return True if the tool should be an MCP server, False for direct."""
    # Hard requirements for MCP
    if needs_claude_desktop or different_language:
        return True

    # Strong MCP preference
    mcp_signals = sum([
        used_by_multiple_agents,  # reuse across agents
        has_credentials,          # isolate secrets
    ])
    if mcp_signals >= 2:
        return True

    # Ambiguous — default to direct for simplicity
    return False

# Examples:
print(should_use_mcp('github_pr_list',
                      used_by_multiple_agents=True,
                      has_credentials=True,
                      needs_claude_desktop=False,
                      different_language=False))  # True

print(should_use_mcp('format_markdown',
                      used_by_multiple_agents=False,
                      has_credentials=False,
                      needs_claude_desktop=False,
                      different_language=False))  # False
blocks from both approaches — the difference is architectural, not in how the model behaves.
Direct function calling is simpler: schema and implementation live in the agent process, credentials in the agent's environment.
MCP adds a separate server process: schemas are discovered dynamically, credentials are isolated per server, and the server works with any MCP host.
The migration path from direct to MCP is mechanical — move the function body, move credentials, replace the dispatch.
Choose direct for single-agent prototypes; choose MCP when a tool should be shared, isolated, or available in Claude Desktop/Code.
